sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 30, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 29 of about 61 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The bridge pattern is the same regardless of player row - it just shifts vertically. The pattern is:
Row N: w, g, w, g, w, g, w, g, w, g, w, g, w
Row N+1: g, g, g, g, g, w, g, g, g, g, g
The bridge pattern doesn't change with player position, it just moves. So the bridge is a fixed pattern that moves with the player.
Let me think about this differently. The bridge has a fixed pattern. The colored blocks (green, blue) are on the bridge. The red block is separate.
Maybe the goal is about the ORDER of blocks on the bridge. Currently, from left to right on the bridge: green, blue. The HUD shows: pink, red, green, blue. Maybe I need to arrange the blocks in a specific order.
Or maybe the goal is about the position of the colored blocks relative to the bridge pattern. The bridge has a specific pattern of white and gray cells. Maybe each colored block needs to be at a specific position on the bridge pattern.
Let me look at where the green and blue blocks are relative to the bridge pattern.
Green is at col 30 (from earlier), blue is at col 36. The bridge pattern at those columns:
- Col 30: row N has g (gray), row N+1 has w (white) - wait, let me re-check the pattern.
Looking at the bridge pattern output:
Row 26: w at 16, g at 17, w at 17-19, g at 20, w at 20-22, g at 23, w at 23-25, g at 26, w at 26-28, g at 29, w at 34, g at 35, w at 40
Row 27: g at 16, g at 19, g at 22, g at 25, g at 28, w at 29, g at 34...
Wait, this is confusing because the segmentation groups adjacent same-color cells. Let me look at the bridge more carefully.
The bridge seems to be a 2-row high structure. The top row (row 26) has: w, g, w, g, w, g, w, g, w, g, w, g, w
The bottom row (row 27) has: g, g, g, g, g, w, g, g, g, g, g...
Actually, I think the bridge is a checkerboard-like pattern. Let me look at it column by column.
Looking at the bridge pattern:
- Col 16: row 26=w, row 27=g
- Col 17: row 26=g, row 27=?
- Col 18: row 26=w (part of 17-19), row 27=?
- Col 19: row 26=w, row 27=g
- Col 20: row 26=g, row 27=?
...
This is getting too complicated. Let me try a different approach. Let me look at the initial state of the game and see what the "goal" state might look like.
Actually, let me try something simple: what if I just need to move the green block to the red block's position? The red block is at (13, 42). The green block is currently at some position. If I move the player to row 13 and then move RIGHT until the green block overlaps with the red block, maybe the level completes.
But earlier I found that the player can't move UP beyond row 18. So the player can't reach row 13.
Wait, let me re-check. The player is currently at row 24-26. Let me try moving UP more to see the actual boundary.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at row 24-26, let me try to move UP to the boundary
# and see exactly where the boundary is
action(['UP', 'UP', 'UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('R', 'b', 'N') and min(p[0] for p in n['boundary']) < 50:
        r = min(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
        c = min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
        print(f"  {n['color']}: ({r}, {c})")
    if n['color'] == 'M' and min(p[0] for p in n['boundary']) < 50:
        r_min = min(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
        print(f"  Player: row {r_min}")
print(f"Board changed: {last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")

# Now try one more UP to see if it works
action(['UP'])
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and min(p[0] for p in n['boundary']) < 50:
        r_min = min(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
        print(f"  Player after UP: row {r_min}")
print(f"Board changed: {last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

  N: (13, 30)
  b: (13, 36)
  R: (13, 42)
  Player: row 14
Board changed: True
  Player after UP: row 12
  Player after UP: row 14
Board changed: False
